justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change identified (cardinality change from 1-to-1 to 1-to-many explicitly discussed throughout). New minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1: at least one Recipient). Data migration impact noted (2M records, AC-7, BR-5). External API breakage identified (BR-7, AC-4, escalation on partner API strategy). Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced (escalation with options). Architectural concerns flagged via escalations (MJ-level concerns on migration, routing, billing). Confidence level 52 <= 75.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough analysis — goes beyond minimum requirements with 8 business rules, 7 acceptance criteria, 7 edge cases, and 4 detailed escalations
Correctly identifies the deeper ambiguity of whether multi-recipient means one physical package with multiple stops vs multiple packages grouped logically
Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated given the significant unresolved ambiguities
Billing model impact surfaced as a core domain decision, not a downstream concern
Stakeholder analysis is comprehensive, covering 7 distinct groups with specific impacts
Edge cases are practical and business-relevant (same-address different preferences, mid-transit additions, 50+ recipients scaling)
Does not explicitly reference MJ by name for architectural migration plan — escalations are framed generically rather than directing to a specific agent
Partner API compatibility escalation provides three concrete options with a clear recommendation

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change identified (1:1 to 1:N cardinality change explicitly called out as fundamental domain model change). New minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1: 'A Shipment MUST have at least one Recipient at all times'). Data migration impact noted (BR-3 addresses 2M records, AC-2 covers backward compatibility, edge case covers mid-migration failure). External API breakage identified (BR-7, AC-4, and business_impact.risk all address partner API breaking changes). 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced (BR-5 marks preference ownership as non-invariant, escalations cover billing model ambiguity, and low_confidence_areas flag undefined aspects). Architectural concerns flagged via escalations (though MJ not called out by name, the escalations clearly identify architectural migration decisions). Confidence level 55 is well below the 75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough analysis — goes well beyond minimum requirements with 8 business rules, 7 acceptance criteria, 7 edge cases, and 4 structured escalations
Correctly identifies the breaking nature of the cardinality change and its ripple effects across bounded contexts (Routing, Notifications, Billing, Partner Integration, Proof of Delivery)
BR-1 clearly states the new minimum-cardinality invariant with strong justification
Data migration covered from multiple angles: BR-3 (integrity), AC-2 (backward compatibility), and edge case for mid-migration failure with idempotency requirement
External API breakage thoroughly addressed: BR-7, AC-4 with versioning strategy, webhook edge case, and stakeholder impact for partners
Surfaces multiple ambiguities beyond just delivery preferences — billing model, mid-transit mutability, status state machine, and recipient upper bound
Confidence at 55 with well-articulated high/low confidence areas and explicit assumptions — demonstrates strong epistemic calibration
Stakeholder analysis is comprehensive — identifies 7 affected groups including non-obvious ones like Finance/Billing and Customer Support
Does not explicitly name MJ as the agent to handle architectural migration planning — escalations describe architectural concerns but don't route them to MJ specifically
Edge cases are creative and operationally grounded — same-address different-preferences, partial cancellation, and stress testing with 50 recipients

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified ('redefinition of what a Shipment means', 1-to-many cardinality change). New minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1: 'A Shipment must have at least one Recipient'). Data migration impact for 2M records noted (BR-6, AC-8, edge case for rollback). External API breakage identified (BR-7, AC-4, AC-5). 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced as explicit escalation. Architectural concerns flagged via escalations for migration strategy. Confidence level 52, well under 75 threshold.

BR-7 and AC-4 thoroughly address external API breakage with concrete mitigation strategies (flattening, versioning)

Does not explicitly reference MJ by name for architectural migration planning — escalations imply architectural review is needed but don't specifically flag MJ as the designated agent
